section_path: Introduction
paragraphs.text: At Apple, our mission is to create technology that empowers people and enriches their lives. We put our users at the center of everything we do, from the way we design our products, services, and software to the policies we support. And we are committed to helping foster a digital economy that enables meaningful and safe experiences for European consumers.

section_path: Introduction
paragraphs.text: As the European Commission continues its preparatory works on the Digital Fairness Act, we wanted to voice our support for many of its policy goals: from protecting the safety of children online, to strengthening transparency and user controls online. We've long believed that users deserve a safe, transparent, and privacy-protecting experience from their technology and the online services they use. And we look forward to continuing to engage with the European Commission as this regulation takes shape.

section_path: Introduction
paragraphs.text: Across our products, we are continuously innovating to set the industry standard for privacy, security, and safety. Since 2008, we have also worked diligently to deliver choice and innovation to European consumers through the App Store, which now offers nearly 2 million apps and provides a unique opportunity for European developers to launch and grow businesses.

section_path: Introduction
paragraphs.text: Our recent submission to the EU Consumer Agenda 2025-30 outlines the many ways in which Apple delivers on user safety, security and privacy by design. Every Apple device combines hardware, software, and services designed to work together for maximum security, privacy and a transparent user experience. We provide tools that empower our users to better understand and manage their personal data and their device usage. The App Store enables our users to make informed decisions before downloading apps, and offers transparency and controls for users, including in relation to subscriptions. Based on our App Review Guidelines, Apple team members review both new apps and updates, working tirelessly to protect our users from harmful business practices and illegal content.

section_path: Introduction
paragraphs.text: While Apple works hard to provide the strongest possible protections to our users, the reality is that consumers continue to face increasingly complex risks when using online services. For years, Apple has publicly warned against the risk of providing data brokers with unfettered access to users' sensitive information, notably to generate hyper-personalized advertising. Tackling unfair personalization practices enabled by dark patterns continues to be as crucial as ever to address such user privacy risks, in order to ensure users are not encouraged to engage in behavior that they otherwise would not have. Despite the huge

section_path: Introduction
paragraphs.text: Empowering users in the digital economy

section_path: Introduction
paragraphs.text: impact on users, we continue to see a lack of coordination between actions driven by competition and platform regulation, on the one hand, and other regulatory and policy needs, including in relation to EU fundamental rights to privacy and security, on the other.

section_path: Introduction
paragraphs.text: The DFA creates an important opportunity to empower consumers in ways that protect their personal data and help them make informed choices across online services. Based on Apple's longstanding commitment to user-centric design, and building on our suggestions for the Consumer Agenda 2025-30, we welcome the opportunity to share our recommendations for the DFA. These include:

section_path: Introduction
paragraphs.text: adopting stronger requirements when it comes to how European consumers engage with trackers, while incentivizing privacy-preserving methods and enhancing innovation;

section_path: Introduction
paragraphs.text: ensuring all digital players deliver on child safety;

section_path: Introduction
paragraphs.text: clarifying and strengthening responsibilities for all actors in the online economy, ensuring that the party closest to the consumer and content is ultimately responsible for delivering consumer rights;

section_path: Introduction
paragraphs.text: ensuring effective collaboration between regulatory authorities;

section_path: Introduction
paragraphs.text: and enabling the digitization of user information required by European law in order to make it user friendly and accessible.

section_path: Introduction
paragraphs.text: Empowering users in the digital economy

section_path: Promoting privacy-enhancing EU consumer laws
paragraphs.text: At Apple, we believe privacy is a fundamental human right. We design all of our products with privacy in mind, and we are continuously working to help users keep their personal data secure. Apple is one of the few US tech companies that has consistently supported the EU's landmark GDPR, before, during and after its passage. When speaking in the European Parliament in 2018, Apple's CEO Tim Cook publicly welcomed the GDPR, calling for other regions in the world to follow the EU's model and voicing concerns over companies prioritizing data monetization. Apple has long supported privacy regulations like the GDPR in providing baseline protections across the board — but those regulations should be a floor, not a ceiling, when it comes to protecting users' privacy.

section_path: Promoting privacy-enhancing EU consumer laws
Apple innovation in privacy controls
paragraphs.text: Apple designs its products and services guided by our four key privacy principles:

section_path: Promoting privacy-enhancing EU consumer laws
Apple innovation in privacy controls
paragraphs.text: Data Minimization: Collecting only the minimum amount of data required to deliver what is needed for a given service.

section_path: Promoting privacy-enhancing EU consumer laws
Apple innovation in privacy controls
paragraphs.text: On-Device Processing: Processing data on the device, wherever possible, rather than sending it to Apple servers, to protect user privacy and minimize data collection.

section_path: Promoting privacy-enhancing EU consumer laws
Apple innovation in privacy controls
paragraphs.text: User Transparency and Control: Making sure that users know what data is shared and how it is used, and that they can exercise control over it.

section_path: Promoting privacy-enhancing EU consumer laws
Apple innovation in privacy controls
paragraphs.text: Security: Hardware and software working together to keep data secure.

section_path: Promoting privacy-enhancing EU consumer laws
Apple innovation in privacy controls
paragraphs.text: We have also given developers more ways to provide greater transparency to their users about the data they're collecting and why. Building on a long history of innovation in privacy controls, we introduced App Tracking Transparency (ATT) in 2020, allowing users to simply and easily decide whether their apps should be able to track their activity across other companies' apps and websites, for example for advertising or sharing with data brokers.

section_path: Promoting privacy-enhancing EU consumer laws
Apple innovation in privacy controls
paragraphs.text: Despite the proven privacy-protective effects and strong support from users and experts alike, some European competition authorities are challenging ATT. They have argued that

section_path: Promoting privacy-enhancing EU consumer laws
Apple innovation in privacy controls
paragraphs.text: Empowering users in the digital economy October 2025

section_path: Promoting privacy-enhancing EU consumer laws
Apple innovation in privacy controls
paragraphs.text: Apple should not be able to show ATT prompts if developers consider that they are also legally required to show GDPR and ePrivacy consent choices.

section_path: Promoting privacy-enhancing EU consumer laws
Apple innovation in privacy controls
paragraphs.text: In fact, ATT does not impact how each app and website obtains user's consent to the processing of their data. Rather, it provides users with a simple, transparent way — a single yes-or-no prompt — to choose whether developers can track their Apple device activity across third-party websites or apps. In a study from the European Commission, ATT has been applauded for its "user-friendly" and "easily accessible" interface, concluding that the high percentage of users choosing to opt out of tracking suggests that "ATT prompts have empowered a significant share of Apple users to indicate their data collection preferences."4

section_path: European consumers face excessive data harvesting practices
paragraphs.text: Many companies take a different approach than Apple. As outlined in our 2021 White Paper, $^{5}$ a large and opaque global industry has for over two decades been increasingly harvesting users' personal data, including by tracking them across an increasingly complex ecosystem of third-party websites and apps without their knowledge and often in ways they don't understand.

section_path: European consumers face excessive data harvesting practices
paragraphs.text: Tracking and developing exact profiles of users has become ubiquitous in the online world. Users' sensitive data is pieced together, shared, aggregated, and monetized to fuel an industry generating hundreds of billions of dollars each year. They use this information to generate hyper-personalized advertising, or track users' locations in the real world so as to build a comprehensive profile of their daily movements.

section_path: European consumers face excessive data harvesting practices
paragraphs.text: A study by the Norwegian Consumer Council showed how the most popular apps track consumers and transferred their data to many different third parties for profiling purposes. The average app has six trackers and single websites have been found to deploy as many as 50 different trackers on a user's device. These stockpiles of personal data create enduring digital profiles of users, which serve only to enrich the companies that collect them.

section_path: European consumers face excessive data harvesting practices
paragraphs.text: Empowering users in the digital economy October 2025

section_path: European consumers face excessive data harvesting practices
paragraphs.text: 4 AWO for the European Commission, see footnote 3.

section_path: European consumers face excessive data harvesting practices
paragraphs.text: 5 "A Day in the Life of your Data", April 2021 - https://www.apple.com/privacy/docs/A_Day_in_the_Life_of_Your_Data.pdf

section_path: European consumers face excessive data harvesting practices
paragraphs.text: 6 Norwegian Consumer Council, "Out of Control - How consumers are exploited by the online advertising industry," January 14, 2020: https://storage02.forbrukerradet.no/media/2020/01/2020-01-14-out-of-control-final-version.pdf

section_path: European consumers face excessive data harvesting practices
paragraphs.text: 7 This figure represents the average, efficiency and speed of browsing. Ghostery, "WhoTracks.me," accessed October 13, 2025

section_path: European consumers face excessive data harvesting practices
paragraphs.text: The current ePrivacy directive has not been sufficient to address the excesses of the online advertising ecosystem, while voluntary efforts to better manage cookie and consent fatigue have largely failed to empower users with more control over how their data is used.8 Consumers are often faced with dark patterns, that offer little if any genuine choice on how their information will be used, and rarely are they fully aware of what they are consenting to.9 Solutions based on centralized opt-out signals have shown to be ineffective, as websites often don't respect consumer choices entered through them.

section_path: European consumers face excessive data harvesting practices
Unintended consequences from other EU regulation
paragraphs.text: We are also concerned about the lack of coordination between regulatory authorities, often to the detriment of user privacy. This is notably the case in relation to actions driven by competition and platform regulation, where the views of data protection authorities and cyber-security agencies were either not sought at all or sought only in a high-level manner that does not allow for the appropriate balance to be struck.

section_path: European consumers face excessive data harvesting practices
Unintended consequences from other EU regulation
paragraphs.text: Apple has consistently voiced concerns around unintended consequences that can flow from new legislation that is enacted without a full assessment of its impact on consumers.10 The Digital Markets Act (DMA) notably lets other companies request access to user data and core technologies of Apple products. We have also been forced by the European Commission to open up access to data that, in order to protect users, even Apple cannot access. For example, the DMA has required Apple to decrypt notifications on user devices and share them with third parties. It has also forced us to share users' full Wi-Fi history, enabling data-hungry companies to create a detailed picture of users' private behavior and patterns online, and to create data-sharing processes that can be abused by determined bad actors. This sets a precedent for large companies to submit new requests to collect even more data in ways that undermine the GDPR's data minimization principles and consent — putting our EU users at much higher risk of surveillance and tracking.

section_path: European consumers face excessive data harvesting practices
Unintended consequences from other EU regulation
paragraphs.text: Empowering users in the digital economy

section_path: Putting privacy at the heart of EU Law
paragraphs.text: The DFA provides an opportunity to put users and their privacy back at the center of EU digital regulation and ensure accountability of the ad tracking industry:

section_path: Putting privacy at the heart of EU Law
paragraphs.text: Unfair and deceptive personalization practices in the online advertising sector must be stopped through clear, enforceable rules, for example by mandating proactive information to consumers on the use of trackers, and by requiring that choices are presented neutrally.

section_path: Putting privacy at the heart of EU Law
paragraphs.text: Consumers can benefit from many new innovative features and services, in such a way that does not create new or more personal data on users. The DFA should incentivize privacy preserving and enhancing methods (such as homomorphic encryption and differential privacy). Privacy enhancing or privacy-by-design practices such as automatic blocking of trackers by default should also be systematically encouraged across online services.

section_path: Putting privacy at the heart of EU Law
paragraphs.text: Any definition of dark patterns must focus on practices aimed at harming consumers or circumventing their rights, and not discourage user interface design decisions, default settings and notifications with the intention to protect users and their privacy. We must not give data harvesters another opportunity to weaponize EU law to their benefit.

section_path: Putting privacy at the heart of EU Law
paragraphs.text: European consumers should, when they choose not to opt in to tracking technologies or not to pay for a specific digital service, be able to enjoy equivalent services, so as to ensure that privacy is not just available to those who can afford it.

section_path: Putting privacy at the heart of EU Law
paragraphs.text: Coordination between regulatory authorities must be strengthened. This is key to enforcement decisions that have far-reaching consequences for EU citizens, which must take into "utmost account" the views of specialized privacy regulators, as reflected in the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) jurisprudence.

section_path: Putting privacy at the heart of EU Law
paragraphs.text: Empowering users in the digital economy October 2025

section_path: Delivering on child safety in digital spaces
paragraphs.text: At Apple we are deeply committed to protecting the safety and privacy of all our users—especially children. We understand that protecting children from online threats requires constant vigilance and effort. The digital world is increasingly complex and the risks to families are ever-changing, including the proliferation of age-inappropriate content and excessive time on social media and other platforms.

section_path: Delivering on child safety in digital spaces
paragraphs.text: Building on Apple's profound commitment to user privacy, security and safety, Apple introduced parental controls for iPhone as early as 2008. In the years since, Apple has worked diligently to add functionalities, tools and policies - including Child Accounts, Family Sharing, and Family Checklist - that help protect and provide an age-appropriate experience for children online while also protecting their privacy. The App Store includes a specific Kids category that makes it easier for parents to identify age-appropriate apps for their families. Apps in the App Store's Kids category may not include external links or in-app purchasing opportunities outside of a designated area behind a parental gate, are prohibited from sharing any data collected with third parties, and require all advertising to be human-reviewed to ensure it meets our standards for appropriate content.

section_path: Delivering on child safety in digital spaces
paragraphs.text: Parental controls are built directly into products like iPhone, iPad, and Mac, and can help families manage the downloads and purchases their kids can make, who they can communicate with, and the apps and other content they can access. This year, we've expanded these tools even further to help parents navigate the risks of an increasingly complex digital world. This includes more granular age ratings, with three ratings to support adolescents, to help ensure that children aged 13 to 17 have age-appropriate protections enabled by default from the time they create their account.

section_path: Delivering on child safety in digital spaces
paragraphs.text: As the serious dangers to kids online continue to grow and evolve, Apple will keep building thoughtful tools and resources to help parents protect their families, and to help everyone do their part to keep kids safe. However, our innovations can only complement the efforts of digital service-providers, who know their content and customers' best. A sound regulatory environment is also therefore crucial to ensure protection across the board. This is why we have been supportive of EU legislation and initiatives that seek to protect children's online safety while ensuring respect for their right to privacy.

section_path: Delivering on child safety in digital spaces
paragraphs.text: The DFA should aim to align the protection of children's rights and the best interests of the child across all online service providers - not just for those services regulated under the Digital Services Act (DSA) or the Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVSMD). These

section_path: Delivering on child safety in digital spaces
paragraphs.text: Empowering users in the digital economy

section_path: Delivering on child safety in digital spaces
paragraphs.text: laws, as well as the GDPR, and the recently adopted European Commission guidelines on Art. 28 DSA, have all contributed to developing an EU approach to child online protection. Ultimately, the DFA should be complementary to existing laws that protect children in the EU, plugging the gaps in this area and avoiding conflicting or overlapping obligations that could lead to a fragmented landscape.

section_path: Delivering on child safety in digital spaces
paragraphs.text: Additionally, the DFA would appear to be the right instrument to address some of the most pressing issues in this space - in particular matters of age assurance for high risk platforms, and the EU-wide minimum age for access to social media. This is particularly important as national governments are increasingly developing local solutions, putting the EU single market at risk. The DFA should therefore define adequate and harmonized means and methods for age assurance across Member States, consistent with the Art. 28 DSA guidelines, as well as the recent European Data Protection Board Opinion on the interplay between the DSA and the GDPR. It is paramount that age assurance requirements be implemented in a flexible framework that takes into account the risk of the service to minors, the impact on user privacy, and other rights and freedoms.

section_path: Delivering on child safety in digital spaces
paragraphs.text: Finally, while platforms should continue to mitigate risks to users under the Digital Services Act, focusing responsibility on the party that is closest to the consumer and content will be key to ensuring clarity for consumers and businesses alike. Service providers know their products, services, and customers best, and are responsible for providing age appropriate experiences. As outlined in prior submissions, age assurance is one area in which service providers can and should be held legally accountable, rather than requiring marketplaces to verify users' ages across the board in a way that compromises their fundamental right to privacy.

section_path: Delivering on child safety in digital spaces
paragraphs.text: Empowering users in the digital economy

section_path: Modernizing and simplifying consumer information through digitization
paragraphs.text: The DFA provides a opportunity to ensure that users better engage with consumer information, while modernizing and simplifying the regulatory environment for products and service providers. Existing requirements to provide paper-based user information notably constitute a challenge to the EU Single Market. These include obligations required by consumer legislation, safety information, product-specific information (such as radio power and frequency bands), charging information, compliance markings, information and markings on end-of-life management, energy efficiency, repair information and labels and more. In 2023, Apple bundled the equivalent of nearly 1 billion sheets of A4 paper with all its products in Europe for regulatory information alone, despite the fact that printed documentation is rapidly discarded by most consumers, often without being read.

section_path: Modernizing and simplifying consumer information through digitization
paragraphs.text: The European Commission should allow companies to provide required consumer-facing information digitally. As policy-makers have recognized, digital information empowers consumers to better engage with the material, as it is easily updated, available in multiple languages and formats, and searchable.

section_path: Modernizing and simplifying consumer information through digitization
paragraphs.text: Digitization ensures that important information for users is made accessible to a broader audience than can be reached with print based media. With digital functionalities such as a voice-over, digital magnifier and zoom, users can increase the size of fonts, increase contrast or have information read out loud. Such functionalities allow people with visual impairments to consume important product information, something that is oftentimes not possible with print-based media. For instance, people with color blindness or differentiation issues can change font and/or background color settings on their device, ensuring the delivery of the information is customized to their specific needs to be clearly readable.

section_path: Modernizing and simplifying consumer information through digitization
paragraphs.text: Finally, digitization ensures that user instructions and important materials are available throughout the product lifecycle, with information that can remain up to date. For companies, it would also make it easier and less costly to adapt to regulatory changes and significantly reduce market entry barriers.

section_path: Modernizing and simplifying consumer information through digitization
paragraphs.text: Empowering users in the digital economy

section_path: Modernizing and simplifying consumer information through digitization
paragraphs.text: At Apple, we are committed to creating technology that empowers people and enriches their lives. That's why we support policies that create transparency for users, preserve people's privacy, keep children safe online, and hold companies accountable for the content on their platforms. We're hopeful that lawmakers will continue to prioritize these values as the Digital Fairness Act takes shape, and we stand ready to share our perspectives to help inform the regulatory process.

section_path: Modernizing and simplifying consumer information through digitization
paragraphs.text: Empowering users in the digital economy
